best technical takeaway today was from the precision livestock meet-up and organized chat. lots of potential for using automated technology to detect and measure welfare for individual farmed animals, even when there are many many packed in a shed or nets (salmon)

potential in measuring their vocalisations and having that as the kpi from regulators instead of specific asks like cage size, enrichment. automated monitoring also makes enforcement of existing welfare regulations (abuse prevention) (now next to nothing) much much easier!

as far as we know, there is no concrete org which wants to develop its own in-house monitoring software/hardware and sell it (@AnimalInnovate "advocate and spec", but don't develop). in theory, this is an opportunity for advocates to take the lead and set measurable welfare

standards that are acceptable to us, not purely for the interests of industry, as long as they are the ones building and providing the new monitoring systems. interestingly, Jay and co. at Electric Sheep https://t.co/gkQYYZsaVG are holding events for prototyping...

So there are similar ideas floating around the area. ubiquitous monitoring, potentially including translation, of animals. the question is if there's the talent and support for that talent to pull everything together: hardware, software, funding and policy?
